disease.name: Vitamin K Deficiency Bleeding (VKDB)
disease.note: Formerly 'hemorrhagic disease of newborn'. Vitamin K deficiency bleeding due to low stores at birth, low breast milk content, and sterile neonatal gut. Three patterns: Early (birth-24h, severe, maternal drugs), Classic (day 2-7, spontaneous GI bleeding, umbilical stump bleeding), Late (week 2-12, intracranial bleed, exclusively breastfed, cholestasis). PREVENTABLE with IM vitamin K prophylaxis at birth.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Late-onset with intracranial bleed -> EMERGENCY; classic VKDB -> URGENT vitamin K + workup

subtypes.name: Early Onset VKDB (birth-24h)
subtypes.notes: Maternal drugs (anticonvulsants, warfarin, TB drugs); severe, cephalohematoma, intracranial

subtypes.name: Classic VKDB (day 2-7)
subtypes.notes: No prophylaxis given; GI bleeding, umbilical stump, circumcision; most common form

subtypes.name: Late Onset VKDB (week 2-12)
subtypes.notes: Breastfed + underlying cholestasis; INTRACRANIAL BLEEDING; mortality 50%

firstLine.test: Apt test (differentiate from swallowed maternal blood)

secondLine.test: Factors II, VII, IX, X (low levels - vitamin K-dependent)
PIVKA-II (elevated - protein induced by vitamin K absence)
Head US/CT (if neurological signs - late onset VKDB)

investigations.goldStandard: Markedly prolonged PT with normal platelet count and normal fibrinogen, plus PIVKA-II elevated

emergency: IV vitamin K 1mg (may repeat in 4-6h if still bleeding - up to 3 doses)
FFP 10-20mL/kg IV if life-threatening bleed (provides clotting factors immediately)
rFVIIa (recombinant factor VIIa) 15-30 mcg/kg if no response to FFP
inpatient: Workup for underlying cause (cholestasis in late onset)
outpatient: Ensure vitamin K prophylaxis at birth for future siblings
Breastfeeding mothers can take oral vitamin K supplements (controversial benefit)
Monitor development if intracranial bleed occurred
preventive: IM vitamin K 1mg at birth for ALL neonates (standard of care, prevents ALL forms)
Oral vitamin K (2mg at birth, then weekly for 12 weeks) if parents refuse IM
Prenatal counseling for mothers on anticonvulsants/anticoagulants
Maternal oral vitamin K 10-20mg daily in last 4 weeks of pregnancy if on anticonvulsants

disease.pathophysiology: Vitamin K is a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase which converts inactive precursors to active clotting factors II, VII, IX, X (and proteins C, S). Without vitamin K, liver produces PIVKA (protein induced by vitamin K absence) - biologically inactive. PT prolonged first (Factor VII half-life 4-6h shortest). Poor placental transfer (cord blood 30-50% adult levels). Breast milk low in vitamin K (1-4 mcg/L). Newborn gut sterile (no bacterial menaquinone synthesis). Late onset: underlying cholestasis prevents absorption of oral vitamin K.

complications.complication: Massive GI bleed with hypovolemic shock

mustNotMiss: VKDB is PREVENTABLE - IV vitamin K at birth is mandatory
Late-onset VKDB presents with seizures/intracranial bleed at 2-12 weeks - think of it
Classic VKDB: day 2-7, GI + umbilical bleeding, NO vitamin K given
Apt test is CRITICAL to differentiate VKDB from swallowed maternal blood
Late-onset almost always has underlying cholestasis - investigate further

diagnosticClues.clue: Neonate with spontaneous GI bleeding + NO vitamin K prophylaxis + markedly prolonged PT
Normal platelet count and fibrinogen (differentiates from DIC)
Infant 2-12 weeks with seizures + exclusively breastfed + cholestasis (late onset)

exclusionClues.clue: Apt test positive for adult Hb (swallowed maternal blood)
Prolonged PTT alone with normal PT (hemophilia)
Low platelets + prolonged PT/PTT + elevated D-dimer (DIC)
Vitamin K already given at birth (consider other coagulopathy)
